cues.title: Watch the motion
cues.text: Watch the two pilots on the glide track. At first, both are still. Now watch their hands. Their palms press, and both platforms begin to move apart. The motion is our first clue. Which two objects are interacting?

cues.title: A force needs two
cues.text: A force is a push or pull during an interaction. The blue pilot pushes on the orange pilot. At the same moment, the orange pilot pushes on the blue pilot. One interaction produces two forces, acting on two different objects.

cues.title: Name both objects
cues.text: Follow each arrow to the object receiving the push. Blue on orange belongs with the orange pilot. Orange on blue belongs with the blue pilot. The arrows are equal in length and point in opposite directions. One interaction; two force arrows.

cues.title: Motion is evidence
cues.text: Compare the evidence before and after contact. Before the push, both platforms are still. Afterwards, they move apart. The change is motion. We do not see a force floating through the air; we identify the interaction and measure what changes.

cues.title: The push has ended
cues.text: Here is an idea worth testing: perhaps the push travels inside each pilot. Check the sequence. While their palms touch, the contact-force arrows are present. When their hands separate, those arrows disappear. The platforms keep gliding, but that contact push has ended.

cues.title: Push or pull
cues.text: Contact forces can push or pull. In the first bay, the robot's padded hand pushes the cart. In the second, the robot pulls the trolley through a taut cable. In both cases, name the pair. One object alone cannot describe an interaction.

cues.title: No touch required
cues.text: Now focus on the gap between the magnets. They do not touch, yet both carts can begin to move. That is evidence of a non-contact interaction. Gravity is another example: Earth pulls objects towards it without making surface contact.

cues.title: Contact or non-contact
cues.text: Use two questions to classify an interaction. First: which two objects are involved? Second: do they touch? A robot hand on a cart is contact. Magnet on magnet is non-contact. Earth pulling an object is non-contact. Strength does not choose the label; touching does.

cues.title: Test one change
cues.text: For a fair comparison, keep the cart, track, and starting line the same. Change only the size of the push, then measure the distance travelled in the same time. If several conditions change together, the evidence cannot tell us which change mattered.

cues.title: Repair the explanation
cues.text: Return to the pilots. Their platforms are still moving after their hands separate. Does that mean a push is stored inside them? No. The contact interaction happened first and changed their motion. After contact ends, the new motion can continue while other forces still act.

cues.title: Predict the evidence
cues.text: Make a prediction before the magnet trial runs. Which observations would support a non-contact interaction? Take three quiet seconds to decide. Now check: the gap remains visible, both carts change motion, and no hand touches either cart. Those observations support the claim.

cues.title: Your physics move
cues.text: Use this physicist's routine whenever you investigate a force. Name both objects. Decide whether they touch. Then use a change in motion as evidence. A force is not hidden material carried by one object. A force belongs to an interaction between objects.

actions.narration: Watch the two pilots on the glide track. At first, both are still. Now watch their hands. Their palms press, and both platforms begin to move apart. The motion is our first clue. Which two objects are interacting?

A force is a push or pull during an interaction. The blue pilot pushes on the orange pilot. At the same moment, the orange pilot pushes on the blue pilot. One interaction produces two forces, acting on two different objects.

Follow each arrow to the object receiving the push. Blue on orange belongs with the orange pilot. Orange on blue belongs with the blue pilot. The arrows are equal in length and point in opposite directions. One interaction; two force arrows.

Compare the evidence before and after contact. Before the push, both platforms are still. Afterwards, they move apart. The change is motion. We do not see a force floating through the air; we identify the interaction and measure what changes.

Here is an idea worth testing: perhaps the push travels inside each pilot. Check the sequence. While their palms touch, the contact-force arrows are present. When their hands separate, those arrows disappear. The platforms keep gliding, but that contact push has ended.

Contact forces can push or pull. In the first bay, the robot's padded hand pushes the cart. In the second, the robot pulls the trolley through a taut cable. In both cases, name the pair. One object alone cannot describe an interaction.

Now focus on the gap between the magnets. They do not touch, yet both carts can begin to move. That is evidence of a non-contact interaction. Gravity is another example: Earth pulls objects towards it without making surface contact.

Use two questions to classify an interaction. First: which two objects are involved? Second: do they touch? A robot hand on a cart is contact. Magnet on magnet is non-contact. Earth pulling an object is non-contact. Strength does not choose the label; touching does.

For a fair comparison, keep the cart, track, and starting line the same. Change only the size of the push, then measure the distance travelled in the same time. If several conditions change together, the evidence cannot tell us which change mattered.

Return to the pilots. Their platforms are still moving after their hands separate. Does that mean a push is stored inside them? No. The contact interaction happened first and changed their motion. After contact ends, the new motion can continue while other forces still act.

Make a prediction before the magnet trial runs. Which observations would support a non-contact interaction? Take three quiet seconds to decide. Now check: the gap remains visible, both carts change motion, and no hand touches either cart. Those observations support the claim.

Use this physicist's routine whenever you investigate a force. Name both objects. Decide whether they touch. Then use a change in motion as evidence. A force is not hidden material carried by one object. A force belongs to an interaction between objects.
